source: Owner's Manual pages 35-40 — extracted via visual inspection of weld diagnosis photos and diagrams

inadequate.visual_description: Cross-section shows weld bead sitting on top of the joint with minimal fusion into the base metal. The weld does not penetrate the joint fully — just on the surface.

proper.visual_description: Cross-section shows weld bead with good fusion into both sides of the joint. Weld is visible underneath and bulges slightly on top. Ideal penetration profile.

excess_burn_through.visual_description: Cross-section shows weld drooping on top and underneath, or falling through entirely making a hole. Excessive fusion has melted through the base material.

example_welds.name: Good Weld
example_welds.visual_description: Smooth, consistent bead with uniform ripple pattern. Even width throughout. Good fusion at edges (toes) with no undercut. Bead profile is slightly convex with smooth transitions to base metal.

example_welds.name: Voltage Too Low or Wire Feed Too Slow
example_welds.visual_description: Tall, narrow, ropey bead that sits high on the surface. Poor wetting at the edges — bead does not spread out. Pronounced ripple pattern with deep valleys. May have a lumpy, piled-up appearance.
example_welds.cause: Insufficient voltage or wire feed speed — not enough heat to properly wet the base metal
example_welds.correction: Increase Output Voltage or Increase Wire Feed Speed

example_welds.name: Voltage Too High or Wire Feed Too Fast
example_welds.visual_description: Wide, flat bead with excessive spatter (small dots scattered around the weld). Irregular edges. May have porosity (small holes). The weld appears spread out and thin with poor definition.
example_welds.cause: Excessive voltage or wire feed speed — too much heat causing spatter and poor bead control
example_welds.correction: Decrease Output Voltage or Decrease Wire Feed Speed

example_welds.name: Travel Speed Too Fast
example_welds.visual_description: Thin, narrow, stringy bead with an elongated appearance. Insufficient fill — the bead looks starved. Poor penetration visible in cross-section. The bead may have a convex, pointed profile.
example_welds.cause: Moving the gun too fast — not enough filler material deposited per unit length

example_welds.name: Travel Speed Too Slow
example_welds.visual_description: Wide, thick, heavily built-up bead with excessive fill. May have a convex, humped profile. Excessive heat input can cause distortion. Bead appears overwelded and wasteful.
example_welds.cause: Moving the gun too slowly — too much filler material deposited

example_welds.name: CTWD Too Long or Wrong Polarity
example_welds.visual_description: Irregular bead with heavy spatter (large scattered droplets surrounding the weld). Poor bead definition. Porosity (holes/cavities) visible. The weld looks messy and inconsistent.
example_welds.cause: Contact Tip to Work Distance is too far, or polarity is incorrect for the process (should be DCEP for MIG, DCEN for Flux-Cored)
example_welds.correction: Check Polarity and maintain less than 1/2" CTWD

porosity.visual_description: Small cavities or holes scattered throughout the weld bead, visible as small round pits on the surface. Like tiny bubbles frozen in metal.
causes: Incorrect polarity — check DCEP for MIG, DCEN for Flux-Cored
Insufficient shielding gas (MIG only) — increase flow, clean nozzle, check for leaks
Incorrect shielding gas (MIG only) — use gas recommended by wire supplier
Dirty workpiece or welding wire — clean to bare metal, ensure wire is free of rust/residues
Inconsistent travel speed — maintain steady travel speed
CTWD too long — reduce CTWD

excessive_spatter.visual_description: Grainy, large droplets of metal scattered around the weld bead. Fine spatter is normal, but large, rough spatter that clings to the workpiece is a problem.
causes: Dirty workpiece or welding wire — clean to bare metal
Incorrect polarity — check DCEP for MIG, DCEN for Flux-Cored
Insufficient shielding gas (MIG only) — increase flow, clean nozzle, maintain proper CTWD
Wire feeding too fast — reduce wire feed speed
CTWD too long — reduce CTWD

crooked_wavy_bead.visual_description: Bead wanders left and right instead of following a straight line. Inconsistent width and direction.
causes: Inaccurate welding — use two hands or rest hand on steady surface
Inconsistent travel speed — maintain steady weld speed
CTWD too long — reduce CTWD

burn_through.visual_description: Base material melts away leaving a hole in the weld. Visible as a gap or opening where the metal has been completely consumed.
causes: Workpiece overheating — reduce current and/or wire feed speed
Travel speed too slow — increase travel speed and keep it steady
Excessive material at weld — reduce wire feed speed

weld_not_adhering.visual_description: Gaps present between weld and previous bead or between weld and workpiece. The weld bead appears to sit on the surface without proper fusion.
causes: Incorrect welding technique — place stringer bead at correct place in joint, keep arc on leading edge of puddle
Insufficient weld heat — increase current, increase wire feed speed
Dirty workpiece — clean to bare metal
Insufficient weld material — increase wire feed speed
Workpiece gap too narrow — widen groove or increase bevel

bend_at_joint.visual_description: Weld bead appears to angle or bend at the joint area rather than following the intended weld path.
causes: Improper clamping — clamp workpieces securely, use tack welds
Excessive heat — weld small portion at a time, increase travel speed, reduce wire feed speed

coat_of_slag.visual_description: Dark, crusty coating covering the weld bead. When partially chipped away, reveals the actual weld underneath.
coat_of_slag.note: Slag is a NECESSARY part of flux-cored wire welding. It shields the weld from impurities. Clean off with chipping hammer and wire brush after welding. Gas-shielded MIG welds do NOT produce slag.

inadequate.visual_description: Cross-section shows weld does not contact the joint fully — just on the surface. Minimal fusion into base metal.

proper.visual_description: Cross-section shows weld visible underneath and bulges slightly on top. Good fusion into both sides.

excess_burn_through.visual_description: Weld droops on top and underneath, or falls through entirely making a hole.

example_welds.name: Good Weld
example_welds.visual_description: Smooth, consistent bead with even ripple pattern. Uniform width. Good tie-in at edges. Proper buildup that is slightly convex.

example_welds.name: Current Too Low
example_welds.visual_description: Tall, narrow, piled-up bead that does not spread. Poor wetting at edges. The bead sits high with inadequate penetration. Lumpy and ropey appearance.

example_welds.name: Current Too High
example_welds.visual_description: Wide, flat, spread-out bead with excessive undercut at the edges. May have spatter. Excessive penetration and possible burn-through.

example_welds.name: Weld Speed Too Fast
example_welds.visual_description: Thin, narrow, stringy bead. Insufficient fill — looks starved. Poor penetration. Elongated ripple pattern.

example_welds.name: Weld Speed Too Slow
example_welds.visual_description: Wide, heavy, over-built bead. Excessive fill with possible distortion of base metal. Overwelded appearance.

example_welds.name: Arc Length Too Short
example_welds.visual_description: Narrow, convex bead with poor wetting. May have inclusions. Bead appears pinched and tall. Electrode may stick to workpiece.
example_welds.cause: Holding the electrode too close to the workpiece
example_welds.correction: Increase distance — hold electrode further from workpiece

example_welds.name: Arc Length Too Long
example_welds.visual_description: Wide, flat, splattery bead with poor definition. Excessive spatter. Porosity may be present. The arc wanders and is difficult to control.
example_welds.cause: Holding the electrode too far from the workpiece
example_welds.correction: Decrease distance — hold electrode closer to workpiece

porosity.visual_description: Small cavities or holes in the bead.
causes: Dirty workpiece or fill material — clean to bare metal, ensure electrode is clean
Inconsistent welding speed — maintain steady speed

excessive_spatter.visual_description: Large, grainy spatter scattered around the weld. Fine spatter is normal.
causes: Dirty workpiece or fill material — clean to bare metal, ensure electrode is clean and free of residues

crooked_wavy_bead.visual_description: Bead wanders and has inconsistent direction.
causes: Inaccurate welding — use two hands or rest hand on steady surface
Inconsistent welding speed — maintain steady speed

burn_through.visual_description: Base material melts away leaving a hole.
causes: Workpiece overheating — reduce current
Welding speed too slow — increase speed and keep steady
Excessive material at weld — reduce amount of fill material

weld_not_adhering.visual_description: Gaps between weld and workpiece or previous bead.
causes: Incorrect welding technique — keep arc on leading edge of puddle, hold at proper angles
Insufficient weld heat — increase current
Dirty workpiece — clean to bare metal
Insufficient weld material — increase amount of fill material
Distance between workpieces to large — decrease distance and increase bevel

bend_at_joint.visual_description: Weld bends at the joint area.
causes: Improper clamping — clamp securely, use tack welds
Excessive heat — weld small portion at a time, increase weld speed

coat_of_slag.visual_description: Dark crusty coating over the weld bead.
coat_of_slag.note: Slag is a necessary part of stick welding. It shields the weld from impurities. Clean off with Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush after welding.
